text:
Union Bank of India – Post-Earnings Concall (FY26 / Q4FY26) Key Takeaways
1) Macro framing & management posture
Management highlighted a risk-aware operating environment since early calendar 2026: “macroeconomic environment has been influenced by ongoing global conflicts and war-related disruptions,” while noting RBI/GoI actions “to put up a cushion” to limit spillovers.
Clear emphasis on balancing growth, liquidity, and profitability, repeatedly framed as “growth with quality… and with profitability,” alongside active balance sheet re-mix to defend margins and improve deposit stability.
2) FY26 financial performance & shareholder return
Net profit: INR 18,697 cr (FY26).
Dividend: INR 5/share (50% of FV), payout ~20.6% (management cited 20.61%/20.65%).
Return metrics:
RoA ~1.25% YoY (flat), but Q4 RoA improved from 1.35% to 1.36% QoQ.
Additional buffer: Bank created INR 700 cr “additional provision, general provision sort of” as a contingency cushion (ECL/geopolitics), and management stressed it is “not impacting either the net profit or the capital.”
3) Balance sheet growth: sharp acceleration in H2, but deposits optically subdued
System-relevant message: growth is being funded by mix-shift + liquidity optimization, not just headline deposits
Management said global business increased by ~INR 1,71,000 cr in six months.
FY26 business growth: ~5.78% YoY; gross advances +9.74% YoY; total deposits +2.72% YoY.
CEO argued deposit growth is understated because the bank also built/used ~INR 46,000 cr of “alternate resources,” broken down as:
INR ~25,000 cr: treasury book shrunk and redeployed to lending
INR ~18,000 cr: refinance/other sources
INR ~3,000 cr: infrastructure bond
CEO’s framing: adding this to deposit increase implies effective resource build ~INR 1.29 lakh cr (~9%+).
Liquidity and funding comfort cited:
CD ratio: “above 80%… comfortable” (explicitly cited ~80.4%, up from ~74/77% in earlier comparisons)
LCR: 114%; NSFR: “much comfortable”
Management reduced excess liquidity: LCR average down from “123… to 114.”

Forward growth target (actionable):

CEO explicitly guided FY27 credit growth expectation of ~13–14% (“Yes, certainly…”).
4) Deposit strategy: deliberate shift away from bulk toward CASA + retail term deposits
Core strategy: “purposefully shifting from bulk deposit to retail term deposit.”
Bulk deposits: “shed off by ~INR 70,000 cr” during the year.
CASA ratio: improved materially from 32.51% (Sep) to 35.21% (Mar), i.e., +2.7 percentage points in ~6 months.
Management linked this to funding-cost advantage: bulk deposits ~“above 7” vs. CASA/retail TD blended “4.5…4.75” (illustrative).
Bulk funding cost disclosure: blended bulk deposit rate for the quarter “around 6.9” (~7).
CDs were used tactically; management noted they raised most requirements in Jan–Feb, avoiding a “heated up” March market for deposits/CDs.
5) Margin (NIM) and NII: pressure acknowledged; management calls a bottom and aims to improve
Reported NIM: declined from 2.91% to 2.70% YoY (CEO: “only 21 bps”), and 2.76% to 2.64% QoQ (“only 12 basis points”).
Primary driver per management: December rate cut passing through EBLR-linked book; “it is basically December rate cut… impact is much lesser than the rate cut.”
Outlook / guidance-like commentary:
CEO expects to defend current levels and turn “positive from here,” adding: “we would like to even better it” vs maintaining 2.64%.
On NII lag vs loan growth in Q4, management attributed it to rate cut transmission and implied normalization: with status quo rates, NII growth should be similar to advances growth (explicitly agreed).
Loan benchmarking mix (important for rate sensitivity):
~54% external benchmark-linked
~36% MCLR
6) Asset quality & credit cost: continued improvement, with monitoring of geopolitical spillover
GNPA: 2.82% (down 78 bps YoY).
NNPA: 0.48% (down 15 bps YoY).
Credit cost: 23 bps for the year; management does not commit to sustain this and instead states a conservative stance: “around 1% we keep as a… guidance.”
SMA commentary: management claimed SMA-2 reduced, with migration to SMA-1 interpreted as improvement: “SMA-2 we have moved… to SMA-1… better proposition.”
West Asia / war monitoring: bank tracking inward remittances, import remittances, hedging; as of call: “we have not seen anything very unusual up till now.”
Impact pockets noted: energy-sensitive clusters (e.g., “Morbi” mentioned).
7) Corporate loan growth, “low-yield” churn, and liquidity trade-offs
Investors challenged the bank on corporate growth vs margin dilution (especially funding with ~6.9% bulk deposits).
Management’s defense: headline growth masks intentional shedding of low-yield assets, including:
“INR 35,000 cr of IBPC… is zero now”
“around INR 30,000 cr… at below 6% levels… almost is not there”
They described ongoing balance sheet churn: replacing short-tenor/low-yield corporate exposures with more profitable/appropriate-tenor assets, while maintaining high underwriting quality and pricing discipline.

Pipeline disclosure (newsworthy):

CEO stated large corporate pipeline ~INR 50,000–60,000 cr.
8) Capital, buffers, and ECL readiness
CRAR: 18.10%; CET1: 15.69% (up from 14.98%).
ECL shortfall: reiterated at ~INR 4,300 cr (“same… INR 4,300”).
Standard asset provisions: “close to INR 2,000 cr.”
Management clarified the INR 700 cr contingency buffer is separate and does not reduce the stated ECL shortfall number in their disclosure framework.
9) One-offs / notable P&L drivers in Q4
Employee cost lower QoQ explained by actuarial/discount rate movement:
Discount rate moved from 7.07 (FY25) to 7.87 (FY26), reducing estimated liability and hence establishment expense in Q4.
Recovery from written-off accounts spiked in Q4:
Q4 TW recovery ~INR 1,600 cr vs INR 666 cr in Q3.
CFO attributed INR 658 cr of this to Sterling Biotech group settlement booked in the quarter.
Operating expenses typically rise in Q4 due to year-end provisioning/budget true-ups (“normal trend… gap of INR 200–300 cr”).
10) Fee income levers: PSLC commentary
PSLC income was soft because activity started only in Q3 this year; earlier year had a fuller period.
Management did not commit to INR 1,000+ cr run-rate; stated they will “assess” based on portfolio/targets but intend to continue.
11) Recoveries / written-off pool
Written-off pool: ~INR 71,000 cr, with ~INR 45,000–46,000 cr under NCLT.
Management expects recovery trend from written-off accounts to continue broadly similarly next year (“most probably, the same trend will be continuing”).
12) Regulatory items: LCR rules & RBI NOP circular
New LCR guidelines impact (bank’s estimate):
Negative impact: ~INR 14,000 cr
Positive impact: ~INR 21,000 cr
Net: ~INR 7,000 cr positive, equating to ~3% LCR benefit for the bank.
RBI NOP circular: “Zero impact”; bank kept FX exposure “only 30 million,” well below the cited 100 million threshold.
Bottom line for investors (from management’s stated stance)
Strategy is explicitly to accelerate loan growth to ~13–14% while defending/improving NIM from ~2.64%, funded by CASA/retail TD ramp + treasury redeployment + selective market borrowings (infra bond/CDs), and supported by strong capital + incremental buffers. Management’s repeated refrain—“we would not be compromising on the quality and also on the profitability”—anchors their response to concerns about corporate growth and funding costs.
